source_dialogue.text: Thank you, Lupita. You've been a big help. I appreciate everything you've done to make my visit go smoothly.

source_dialogue.speaker: Lupita
source_dialogue.text: It's my pleasure. I want to make sure that you have everything you need while you're here. Let me know if there's anything else I can do for you.

source_dialogue.speaker: Boss
source_dialogue.text: Actually, there is one thing...

source_dialogue.speaker: Lupita
source_dialogue.text: Sure, anything. What is it?

source_dialogue.speaker: Boss
source_dialogue.text: Could you please book me a massage at the spa tomorrow morning? That would be great.

source_dialogue.speaker: Lupita
source_dialogue.text: Absolutely! Consider it done boss.

scene.narrative_en: Lupita went to great lengths to make sure his boss's every need was met while she was in town. He arranged for her transportation, set up meeting times with key personnel, and even made sure her hotel room was comfortable. Lupita's boss felt appreciated and grateful for all that he had done.

mapped_refs.notes: common Korean female name used to address a helper
Koreans commonly refer to a spa as '스파'.
Commonly used term for spa massage in Korean.

source_dialogue.speaker: Sandi
source_dialogue.text: Hey, Sarah. I'm sorry to bother you, but I was wondering if you could help me find my keys. I can't remember where I left them and I'm starting to get really frustrated.

source_dialogue.speaker: Sarah
source_dialogue.text: Of course, Sandi. Where did you last see them?

source_dialogue.speaker: Sandi
source_dialogue.text: The last place I remember having them was in my car, but I could have left them anywhere since then. I just don't know where to start looking.

source_dialogue.speaker: Sarah
source_dialogue.text: Well, let's start with your car then. Maybe they're just hidden somewhere and you haven't noticed them yet.

source_dialogue.speaker: Sandi
source_dialogue.text: Okay, that makes sense. Thank you, Sarah. I really appreciate your help.

source_dialogue.speaker: Sarah
source_dialogue.text: No problem at all.

speakers.speech_style_notes: Polite but increasingly frustrated, uses hesitant phrasing.
Calm and supportive, uses gentle questioning.

scene.narrative_en: Sandi is irresponsible and forgetful. She is always losing her things and can never remember where she put them. This causes a lot of problems for her, and she is always having to ask other people for help.

source_dialogue.speaker: Camdyn
source_dialogue.text: I'm thinking about moving to New York City.

source_dialogue.speaker: Father
source_dialogue.text: Why do you want to move there?

source_dialogue.speaker: Camdyn
source_dialogue.text: I don't know. I just feel like I need a change.

source_dialogue.speaker: Father
source_dialogue.text: Have you ever been there before?

source_dialogue.speaker: Camdyn
source_dialogue.text: No, but I've always wanted to go.

source_dialogue.speaker: Father
source_dialogue.text: It's a big city. Are you sure you're ready for that?

source_dialogue.speaker: Camdyn
source_dialogue.text: I'm not sure, but I think so.

source_dialogue.speaker: Father
source_dialogue.text: Well, if you're set on moving, then I support you. Just be careful and stay safe.

scene.narrative_en: Camdyn was born in 1990 in a small town in Maine. She grew up close to the ocean and loved to go fishing with her father. After high school, she attended college for a year before deciding to move to New York City.

mapped_refs.notes: 서울 is the major Korean metropolis that serves as the cultural and economic counterpart to New York City.

source_dialogue.speaker: Analyse
source_dialogue.text: Hey, Sarah. I made pasta for dinner tonight and it turned out really well! I was following a recipe to the letter and it paid off.

source_dialogue.speaker: Sarah
source_dialogue.text: That's great to hear, Analyse! What kind of pasta dish did you make?

source_dialogue.speaker: Analyse
source_dialogue.text: I made a simple spaghetti with tomato sauce. It was really good, and I'm so happy that it turned out well.

source_dialogue.speaker: Sarah
source_dialogue.text: That sounds delicious! I'm glad to hear that your cooking experiment was a success.

speakers.speech_style_notes: Excited, uses exclamation, mentions following a recipe.
Polite, asks follow‑up question, shows enthusiasm.

scene.narrative_en: Analyse had some experience with cooking. She decided to begin by cooking a simple dish, like pasta. She followed the recipe to the letter, and it turned out great!

mapped_refs.notes: A popular Korean noodle soup dish served as a main course in social meals.
Koreans commonly refer to pasta dishes like spaghetti simply as 파스타
고추장은 파스타와 비슷한 조미료 역할을 하는 한국식 양념입니다.

source_dialogue.speaker: Jeremey
source_dialogue.text: Hey Jeannie, how's it going?

source_dialogue.speaker: Jeannie
source_dialogue.text: Hey Jeremey! I'm doing good, just struggling a little bit with this math homework.

source_dialogue.speaker: Jeremey
source_dialogue.text: Sure, I can help you with that. Let me take a look.

source_dialogue.speaker: Jeannie
source_dialogue.text: Thanks so much, Jeremey! I really appreciate it.

source_dialogue.speaker: Jeremey
source_dialogue.text: No problem, I'm happy to help. So what seems to be the issue?

source_dialogue.speaker: Jeannie
source_dialogue.text: I don't really understand this concept of factoring. Can you explain it to me?

source_dialogue.speaker: Jeremey
source_dialogue.text: Yeah, no problem. So basically when you're factoring an equation, you're looking for any common factors between the terms in the equation.

source_dialogue.speaker: Jeannie
source_dialogue.text: Okay, that makes sense. Can you give me an example?

source_dialogue.speaker: Jeremey
source_dialogue.text: Sure. Let's say we have the equation 4x^2 + 12x + 9. To factor this equation, we would first look for any common factors between the terms. In this case, the only common factor is 3x. So we would factor out 3x and be left with 3x(4x+4)+1=0

source_dialogue.speaker: Jeannie
source_dialogue.text: Okay, I think I understand now. Thanks so much for your help!

scene.narrative_en: Jeremey is always willing to help Jeannie with her work. She is happy to answer any questions that Jeannie has and she makes sure that she understands the material. Jeremey is a great tutor and friend.

mapped_refs.notes: Commonly used for school assignments/homework in Korean
수학적 인수분해
Korean students refer to a mathematical equation as 방정식.
한국 수학 수업에서 흔히 쓰는 ‘공통인수’ 개념
표현 자체가 완전제곱식으로, 중학교 수학에서 자주 등장한다

source_dialogue.speaker: Nyasia
source_dialogue.text: Hey, Mom.

source_dialogue.speaker: Mom
source_dialogue.text: Hey, baby. How are you feeling?

source_dialogue.speaker: Nyasia
source_dialogue.text: I'm feeling a little bit excited and nervous. But I know that I am brave and can handle whatever comes my way.

source_dialogue.speaker: Mom
source_dialogue.text: That's great, sweetheart! I'm proud of you for being so positive. School is a big adventure, but you're going to do just fine. You're a smart and strong young man. Just remember to be yourself and have fun!

source_dialogue.speaker: Nyasia
source_dialogue.text: Thanks, Mom. I'll try my best!

source_dialogue.speaker: Mom
source_dialogue.text: I know you will. I love you no matter what.

source_dialogue.speaker: Nyasia
source_dialogue.text: I love you too, Mom.

speakers.speech_style_notes: Young, earnest, slightly nervous but hopeful tone.
Warm, affectionate, reassuring tone with gentle encouragement.

scene.narrative_en: Nyasia is a young boy who is about to embark on his first day of school. He is feeling a mixture of emotions, including excitement and nerves. However, he knows that he is brave and can handle whatever comes his way.

mapped_refs.notes: Affectionate address used by parents for children

source_dialogue.speaker: Hannah
source_dialogue.text: I'm sorry, Mom. I know I messed up. I was wrong and I want to make things right.

source_dialogue.speaker: Mom
source_dialogue.text: Hannah, I love you no matter what you do but sometimes your choices hurt me. You're not the only one who's hurting here. Your sister is too.

source_dialogue.speaker: Hannah
source_dialogue.text: I know, Mom. And I'm sorry. It wasn't just about me. It was about her too and how much she means to me.

source_dialogue.speaker: Mom
source_dialogue.text: What did you do?

source_dialogue.speaker: Hannah
source_dialogue.text: We got into a fight and she said some really hurtful things to me so I lashed out at her and said some things I shouldn't have said back.

source_dialogue.speaker: Mom
source_dialogue.text: So you hit her?

source_dialogue.speaker: Hannah
source_dialogue.text: Yeah, but it was just once and it wasn't that hard. Just a little tap on the shoulder really but she started crying and ran off to her room sobbing uncontrollably. All of this is my fault and if there's anything I can do to make it up to her or to you, just name it and I'll do it

scene.narrative_en: Hannah knew he had messed up when he saw the look on his mother's face. He was sorry for what he had done and wanted to make things right. Hannah hugged his mother and apologized for the pain he had caused her.

source_dialogue.speaker: Brittaney
source_dialogue.text: Hey, Uncle Joe.

source_dialogue.speaker: Uncle Joe
source_dialogue.text: Hey there, Brittaney! How are you doing today?

source_dialogue.speaker: Brittaney
source_dialogue.text: I'm doing alright. Just trying to stay positive like you always say.

source_dialogue.speaker: Uncle Joe
source_dialogue.text: That's the right attitude! You'll get through whatever challenges come your way if you keep that outlook.

source_dialogue.speaker: Brittaney
source_dialogue.text: Yeah, I know. It's just hard sometimes when things seem so bleak. But I guess it could always be worse, right?

source_dialogue.speaker: Uncle Joe
source_dialogue.text: Absolutely! As long as you remember that things could always be worse, you'll be able to appreciate the good moments more too.

source_dialogue.speaker: Brittaney
source_dialogue.text: Thanks, Uncle Joe. You always know just what to say to make me feel better.

source_dialogue.speaker: Uncle Joe
source_dialogue.text: That's because I care about you, Brittaney. You're a good kid with a lot of potential. I just want to see you succeed in life.

source_dialogue.speaker: Brittaney
source_dialogue.text: I appreciate that, Uncle Joe. Thanks for being here for me.

speakers.speech_style_notes: Speaks thoughtfully, uses encouraging language, moderate pace.
Speaks warmly, uses uplifting phrasing, gentle tone.

scene.narrative_en: Brittaney finds a positive role model in his Uncle Joe. Uncle Joe is always so encouraging and has such a great outlook on life, which helps Brittaney to feel encouraged too.

source_dialogue.speaker: Jahziel
source_dialogue.text: You're really upset about this, aren't you?

source_dialogue.speaker: Partner
source_dialogue.text: Yes, I am. I can't believe you would just do something like that without even apologizing. It was really hurtful.

source_dialogue.speaker: Jahziel
source_dialogue.text: I'm sorry if I hurt you, but I don't feel like apologizing is necessary. I didn't do anything wrong and I don't see why saying sorry would be helpful.

source_dialogue.speaker: Partner
source_dialogue.text: Well, it would show that you're willing to take responsibility for your actions and that you care about my feelings. Otherwise, it just feels like you don't care about me or our relationship.

source_dialogue.speaker: Jahziel
source_dialogue.text: I do care about you and our relationship. I'm just not going to apologize for something I didn't do wrong.

source_dialogue.speaker: Partner
source_dialogue.text: This is really frustrating. I feel like you're just being selfish and stubborn.

source_dialogue.speaker: Jahziel
source_dialogue.text: Maybe you're being too sensitive. I'm not going to apologize, so you'll just have to get over it.

speakers.speech_style_notes: Speaks in a steady, unapologetic tone, rarely conceding.
Speaks with rising frustration, uses direct emotional language.

scene.narrative_en: Jahziel never says sorry because they feel like it would be a loss of control. They would rather be seen as strong and in charge, even if it means making others upset. This often leads to conflict and resentment from those around Jahziel.

source_dialogue.speaker: Brentlee
source_dialogue.text: Hey, Landyn. Are you okay?

source_dialogue.speaker: Landyn
source_dialogue.text: No, I'm not okay. Those kids just beat me up for no reason.

source_dialogue.speaker: Brentlee
source_dialogue.text: Why would they do that? Do you know them?

source_dialogue.speaker: Landyn
source_dialogue.text: No, I don't know them. They were just bullying me because they could.

source_dialogue.speaker: Brentlee
source_dialogue.text: That's really messed up. I'm sorry that happened to you.

source_dialogue.speaker: Landyn
source_dialogue.text: Yeah, me too.

scene.narrative_en: Brentlee was walking home from school when he saw Landyn getting beat up by a group of kids. Brentlee didn't know what to do, so he just stood there and watched. After a few minutes, he started to feel sick to his stomach, so he turned around and walked the other way.

source_dialogue.speaker: Edric
source_dialogue.text: So, I've been pretty busy lately. I've been cooking and cleaning my apartment, running errands around town, and studying for my upcoming exams. It's been a lot, but I'm feeling pretty good about everything. How have you been?

source_dialogue.speaker: Sarah
source_dialogue.text: I've been good! A little busy with work, but nothing too crazy. I did want to ask you about your exams though. Are you feeling prepared?

source_dialogue.speaker: Edric
source_dialogue.text: Yeah, I think so. I've been studying a lot and I feel like I have a good handle on the material. I'm just hoping that everything goes well on test day.

source_dialogue.speaker: Sarah
source_dialogue.text: I'm sure you'll do great! You're always so prepared for everything. Let me know if you need any help with studying though, I'd be happy to help out.

scene.narrative_en: Edric has been busy because Edric wants to be productive. She's been cooking and cleaning her apartment, running errands around town, and studying for her upcoming exams.

source_dialogue.speaker: Jaycee
source_dialogue.text: Juan, I don't know how much longer I can keep this up. I'm always working, and I never have any time for myself. I feel like a machine.

source_dialogue.speaker: Juan
source_dialogue.text: I know you're always busy, mi amor, but you're doing an amazing job. We appreciate everything you do for us.

source_dialogue.speaker: Jaycee
source_dialogue.text: I appreciate your words, Juan, but they don't change the fact that I'm exhausted. I never have a moment to just relax and recharge. I feel like I'm running on empty all the time.

source_dialogue.speaker: Juan
source_dialogue.text: Maybe we can talk about this more and see if there's anything we can do to help lighten your load. You deserve to have some time for yourself too, Jaycee.

source_dialogue.speaker: Jaycee
source_dialogue.text: That would be great, thank you. Just knowing that you're willing to help makes me feel a little better.

source_dialogue.speaker: Juan
source_dialogue.text: Of course, anything for you, my love.

speakers.speech_style_notes: Warm, reassuring, uses terms of endearment.

scene.narrative_en: Jaycee is always working. No matter what time of day it is, she can be found working on something. Whether it's cleaning the house, cooking dinner, or doing laundry, she's always busy.

mapped_refs.notes: Affectionate spousal address similar to 'mi amor'.

source_dialogue.speaker: Albert
source_dialogue.text: Hey, stop it! Leave him alone!

source_dialogue.speaker: Nico
source_dialogue.text: Thank you. I appreciate it.

source_dialogue.speaker: Albert
source_dialogue.text: No problem. I can't stand to see anyone getting picked on like that. It's just not right.

source_dialogue.speaker: Nico
source_dialogue.text: Yeah, I know how you feel. I've been bullied myself before.

source_dialogue.speaker: Albert
source_dialogue.text: Really? That sucks. No one deserves to be treated like that.

source_dialogue.speaker: Nico
source_dialogue.text: Tell me about it. It's not a lot of fun, that's for sure. But thanks to people like you who are willing to stand up for others, it makes it a lot easier to get through tough times like this.

source_dialogue.speaker: Albert
source_dialogue.text: You're welcome. I'm just glad I could help. We should stick together and look out for each other, so no one has to go through what you did alone.

source_dialogue.speaker: Nico
source_dialogue.text: Absolutely. Thanks again, Albert.

speakers.speech_style_notes: Direct, supportive, and calm when confronting injustice.

scene.narrative_en: Albert was sitting in class when Nico got bullied. Albert felt bad for Nico, so she intervened and told the bully to stop. The bully stopped, and Nico was saved from getting hurt.

source_dialogue.speaker: Casey
source_dialogue.text: Hey, Addy! Check out my new outfit!

source_dialogue.speaker: Dariel
source_dialogue.text: Wow, you look great! Where did you find it?

source_dialogue.speaker: Casey
source_dialogue.text: I just went to the mall and picked it up. I'm so happy I was able to find something I love and that you approve of.

source_dialogue.speaker: Dariel
source_dialogue.text: Well, of course I approve! You have great taste.

speakers.speech_style_notes: Excited, uses exclamation, direct about outfit.
Warm, supportive, uses admiration, casual tone.

scene.narrative_en: Casey shows Dariel her new outfit and Dariel compliments her on how stylish she looks. Casey is happy that she was able to find an outfit that she loves and that Dariel approves of.

mapped_refs.notes: 백화점은 쇼핑몰과 비슷한 대형 쇼핑 공간을 의미한다
Casual Korean vocative equivalent of “hey”

source_dialogue.speaker: Kaya
source_dialogue.text: I'm not sure what to do with the rest of my money. I was thinking about investing it, but I'm not sure if that's the right decision.

source_dialogue.speaker: Financial Advisor
source_dialogue.text: There are a few things you could do with the money. You could invest it and try to grow your wealth, or you could use it to pay off debts or expenses. It really depends on your financial goals and what you want to achieve with the money.

source_dialogue.speaker: Kaya
source_dialogue.text: I'm not sure what my goals are. I just want to make sure that I'm doing the right thing with my money.

source_dialogue.speaker: Financial Advisor
source_dialogue.text: It sounds like you want to grow your wealth, but you're not sure how to go about it. Investing is a good way to grow your wealth, but there are other options as well. You could use the money to pay off debts or expenses, or you could save it for a future goal. It really depends on what you want to achieve with the money and what your financial goals are.

scene.narrative_en: Kaya had $250,000. Kaya used $200,000 to buy a house. Kaya had $50,000 left.

source_dialogue.speaker: Esther
source_dialogue.text: So my best friend died in a car accident about six months ago. It was really sudden and unexpected, and I still haven't been able to process it or move on. I just feel so stuck. Do you think seeing a therapist could help me feel better?

source_dialogue.speaker: Therapist
source_dialogue.text: Absolutely. Grief can be extremely overwhelming and difficult to cope with, especially when it's sudden and unexpected. Seeing a therapist can help you work through your grief in a healthy way, and hopefully start to move on.

source_dialogue.speaker: Esther
source_dialogue.text: Yeah, I really hope so. I just feel like I'm stuck in this dark place and I can't get out.

source_dialogue.speaker: Therapist
source_dialogue.text: That sounds very difficult. Can you tell me more about how you've been feeling since your friend's death?

source_dialogue.speaker: Esther
source_dialogue.text: Well, like I said, I just feel really stuck. It's like I'm in this dark hole that I can't get out of. Every day feels the same—I just wake up feeling sad and lost. And angry, too. I'm really angry at my friend for dying and leaving me behind.

source_dialogue.speaker: Therapist
source_dialogue.text: It's understandable that you would feel all of those things. Grief is a complex emotion, and it can be very confusing and difficult to deal with.

source_dialogue.speaker: Esther
source_dialogue.text: Yeah, it definitely is. Even though it's been six months, it feels like it happened yesterday sometimes. The pain is just so fresh and raw.

source_dialogue.speaker: Therapist
source_dialogue.text: That makes a lot of sense. Grief can be very unpredictable—you might have good days where you feel like you're making progress, and then suddenly something will happen to trigger all the feelings again and set you back weeks or even months.

source_dialogue.speaker: Esther
source_dialogue.text: Yeah, that's definitely been happening to me lately. Just when I think I'm starting to feel better, something will happen—like seeing someone who looks like my friend or hearing her favorite song on the radio—and it'll all come rushing back again and knock me down again

speakers.speech_style_notes: Slow, reflective, emotive speech with pauses.

scene.narrative_en: Esther's best friend died in a car accident. Now she wants to feel better, so she is going to see a therapist.

mapped_refs.notes: Commonly used to refer to a close friend, akin to 'best friend'.
Koreans typically refer to fatal traffic crashes as 교통사고

source_dialogue.speaker: Clarisa
source_dialogue.text: Hey Harmoni, what's up?

source_dialogue.speaker: Harmoni
source_dialogue.text: Not much, just hanging out. What about you?

source_dialogue.speaker: Clarisa
source_dialogue.text: Same here. Just chilling.

source_dialogue.speaker: Harmoni
source_dialogue.text: So what's new?

source_dialogue.speaker: Clarisa
source_dialogue.text: I don't know, what's new with you?

source_dialogue.speaker: Harmoni
source_dialogue.text: I don't know either. Just the same old stuff I guess.

speakers.speech_style_notes: Casual, upbeat, uses filler words like 'just'.
Laid‑back, uses filler 'I guess', repeats phrases.

scene.narrative_en: Clarisa hangs out with Harmoni because Harmoni is his best friend. Clarisa likes spending time with Harmoni because they always have a good time together.

source_dialogue.speaker: Dandre
source_dialogue.text: Sarah, I've been thinking about moving to a new city. I'm not sure where yet, but I'm just feeling really restless and like I need a change. What do you think?

source_dialogue.speaker: Sarah
source_dialogue.text: Wow, that's a big decision! Have you given any thought to where you might want to go or what kind of job you would get there?

source_dialogue.speaker: Dandre
source_dialogue.text: I haven't really thought too much about it yet, to be honest. I just know that I'm ready for something different. I'm tired of my current job and the same old routine day after day. Plus, all my friends are getting married and having kids, so it feels like everyone's moving on with their lives while I'm stuck in the same place.

source_dialogue.speaker: Sarah
source_dialogue.text: I can understand that. It can be really tough feeling like you're the only one not moving forward in life. But changing everything all at once can be pretty overwhelming, so maybe you should take some time to think about it and figure out what exactly you want before you make any big decisions.

source_dialogue.speaker: Dandre
source_dialogue.text: Yeah, I know. It's just hard when I feel like I'm stuck in a rut and don't know how to get out of it. But I appreciate your advice and will definitely think about it more before making any decisions. Thanks, Sarah!

speakers.speech_style_notes: Thoughtful, slightly hesitant, uses filler words, informal.
Warm, reassuring, uses reflective listening, informal.

scene.narrative_en: Dandre thought about moving. She would have to find a new job, make new friends, and learn her way around a new city. The idea of starting over was both exciting and scary to her.

mapped_refs.notes: 도시는 한국에서 일반적으로 쓰이는 도시라는 의미다
일자리를 찾는 상황

source_dialogue.speaker: Ensley
source_dialogue.text: Why did you feel embarrassed and scared when you had to wear your mother's old clothes to school?

source_dialogue.speaker: Julieanna
source_dialogue.text: I felt embarrassed because I knew that the other kids would make fun of me for wearing old clothes. And I was scared because I didn't want to stand out from the rest of the kids.

source_dialogue.speaker: Ensley
source_dialogue.text: It must have been hard for you to go through that.

source_dialogue.speaker: Julieanna
source_dialogue.text: It was really tough. I felt like I didn't belong anywhere. But my mom always told me that I was just as good as anyone else, no matter what I was wearing.

source_dialogue.speaker: Ensley
source_dialogue.text: That's really sweet of her. Did it make you feel better?

source_dialogue.speaker: Julieanna
source_dialogue.text: Yeah, it did. I realized that my mom loved me no matter what and that made me feel a lot better about myself.

speakers.speech_style_notes: Asks direct, neutral questions with a calm, inquisitive tone.
Speaks reflectively, sharing personal feelings with a gentle cadence.

scene.narrative_en: Julieanna tells Ensley a story about a time when she was very young. Julieanna remembers feeling embarrassed and scared when she had to wear her mother's old clothes to school. Julieanna explains that she had no other choice because her family was very poor.

mapped_refs.notes: Refers to schoolmates, the typical Korean term for peers in a school setting.

source_dialogue.speaker: Eliab
source_dialogue.text: Hi, friend. I'm sorry if I seem a bit off today, I've just been carrying around a lot of heavy emotions and needed to talk to someone about it.

source_dialogue.speaker: Friend
source_dialogue.text: Of course, Eliab. What's going on? You can tell me anything.

source_dialogue.speaker: Eliab
source_dialogue.text: Well, where do I even start...I guess it all began when my mom died a few years ago. It was sudden and unexpected, and it hit me really hard. I felt like I had no one to turn to for support or comfort. My dad was struggling with his own grief and wasn't able to be there for me the way I needed him to be. So, I ended up bottling everything up inside and didn't really deal with my pain in a healthy way.

source_dialogue.speaker: Friend
source_dialogue.text: That sounds really tough, Eliab. I'm so sorry you had to go through that alone. No one should have to deal with something like that by themselves. Did you ever talk to anyone else about it? A therapist or counselor maybe? 	 Or reach out to any other family members or friends for support? 	 It sounds like you've been carrying this all by yourself for a long time now. That can't be easy. Eliab nods her head in response*

source_dialogue.speaker: Eliab
source_dialogue.text: Yeah, I've tried to talk to people about it, but it's just so hard to open up about something like this. And I know that bottling everything up isn't healthy, but I just can't seem to let it go. It feels like if I do, then I'll be admitting that my mom is really gone and I'm never going to see her again. And I'm just not ready to do that yet.

source_dialogue.speaker: Friend
source_dialogue.text: Eliab, it's okay to grieve and it's okay to feel pain. There is no right or wrong way to do it. You are allowed to grieve in whatever way you need to in order to heal. Maybe try journaling or writing down your thoughts and feelings? That might help you process things in a healthier way than keeping everything bottled up inside.

source_dialogue.speaker: Eliab
source_dialogue.text: Yeah, maybe you're right. I think I might give that a try. Thanks for listening, friend. It really means a lot.

source_dialogue.speaker: Friend
source_dialogue.text: Of course, Eliab. Anytime you need someone to talk to, don't hesitate to reach out to me.

speakers.speech_style_notes: Speaks slowly, uses personal storytelling and reflective language.
Offers gentle, affirming responses and supportive advice.

scene.narrative_en: Eliab had a lot of bottled up emotions and needed to tell someone about her experiences. She felt better after talking to her friend about everything that had been going on.

source_dialogue.speaker: Brystal
source_dialogue.text: Hey, Jesse. I found this notepad in the woods and I was thinking about using it to write down some ideas for a new story.

source_dialogue.speaker: Jesse
source_dialogue.text: That sounds like a great idea! What kind of story are you thinking about?

source_dialogue.speaker: Brystal
source_dialogue.text: Well, I was thinking about a story where a group of friends go on an adventure together.

source_dialogue.speaker: Jesse
source_dialogue.text: That sounds like a lot of fun! What kind of adventure are they going on?

source_dialogue.speaker: Brystal
source_dialogue.text: I was thinking maybe they could go camping in the woods and explore some caves or something.

source_dialogue.speaker: Jesse
source_dialogue.text: That sounds like an awesome adventure! I can't wait to read it when you're finished.

scene.narrative_en: Brystal finds a notepad in the woods. The notepad is brand new and has never been used before. Brystal decides to use the notepad to write down some ideas he has for a new story.

mapped_refs.notes: 숲 is the everyday Korean word for a wooded area.

source_dialogue.speaker: Garry
source_dialogue.text: What the hell is going on around here? I thought I told you to get this place in order!

source_dialogue.speaker: Bob
source_dialogue.text: I'm sorry, boss. We've been having some trouble with the employees lately. They just don't seem to be following orders like they used to.

source_dialogue.speaker: Garry
source_dialogue.text: Well, you need to get them in line or I'll find someone who will! This place is a mess and it's all your fault!

source_dialogue.speaker: Bob
source_dialogue.text: Yes, boss. I'll take care of it right away.

scene.narrative_en: The baton was passed to Garry who then became the boss. He ran the show with an iron fist, making sure that everything was done his way. No one dared to cross him for fear of being on the receiving end of his wrath.

source_dialogue.speaker: Louella
source_dialogue.text: I made 150 copies today.

source_dialogue.speaker: Boss
source_dialogue.text: You made 150 copies today?

source_dialogue.speaker: Louella
source_dialogue.text: Yes, I did. I used the copy machine in my office to make them.

source_dialogue.speaker: Boss
source_dialogue.text: Why did you make so many copies?

source_dialogue.speaker: Louella
source_dialogue.text: I needed them for my work.

source_dialogue.speaker: Boss
source_dialogue.text: What work do you do that requires so many copies?

source_dialogue.speaker: Louella
source_dialogue.text: I work in the marketing department, and we often need to make a lot of copies of documents.

source_dialogue.speaker: Boss
source_dialogue.text: That makes sense. Thank you for telling me.

speakers.speech_style_notes: Measured, factual, slightly defensive when questioned.

scene.narrative_en: Louella makes copies of documents all day. She uses the copy machine in her office to make at least one hundred copies every day. Today, she made 150 copies.

mapped_refs.notes: The common Korean term for a copy machine used in offices.
Korean companies often use 홍보팀 for promotion and marketing functions.

source_dialogue.speaker: Marquice
source_dialogue.text: Hey, Sarah. How was church today?

source_dialogue.speaker: Sarah
source_dialogue.text: It was good. I'm really enjoying it.

source_dialogue.speaker: Marquice
source_dialogue.text: Yeah, me too. I'm glad we decided to start going together.

source_dialogue.speaker: Sarah
source_dialogue.text: Yeah, me too.

source_dialogue.speaker: Marquice
source_dialogue.text: I feel like I'm finally starting to get a better understanding of what religion is all about.

source_dialogue.speaker: Sarah
source_dialogue.text: Yeah, me too. I think going to church every week is really helping us both grow spiritually.

speakers.speech_style_notes: Warm, upbeat tone with frequent affirmations.

scene.narrative_en: Marquice has started attending church every Sunday. She's been wanting to be a better person and live her life more religiou

mapped_refs.notes: 종교 예배가 열리는 공동체 공간
Koreans use the word 종교 to refer to religion.

source_dialogue.speaker: Celestina
source_dialogue.text: Hey, thanks for coming by. I really appreciate it.

source_dialogue.speaker: Person with money
source_dialogue.text: No problem, I'm happy to help out. So show me around, what do you have for sale?

source_dialogue.speaker: Celestina
source_dialogue.text: Well, we've got a lot of great stuff here. Let me show you some of our best items.

source_dialogue.speaker: Person with money
source_dialogue.text: Wow, this is all really great stuff! You have a great eye for picking out quality items. How much did you say this piece was again?

source_dialogue.speaker: Celestina
source_dialogue.text: That one is $200.

source_dialogue.speaker: Person with money
source_dialogue.text: I'll take it! And that vase too. How much was that?

source_dialogue.speaker: Celestina
source_dialogue.text: That one is $100.

source_dialogue.speaker: Person with money
source_dialogue.text: I'll take it as well. Thanks so much for your help, Celestina.

scene.narrative_en: Celestina felt proud when she was able to show the person with the money around. She had been working hard and it felt good to be able to help this person out.

source_dialogue.speaker: Madalynn
source_dialogue.text: Thanks for being here, Gail. It really means a lot to me.

source_dialogue.speaker: Gail
source_dialogue.text: Of course, Madalynn. I'm always here for you. You know that.

source_dialogue.speaker: Madalynn
source_dialogue.text: I know. But it's just been really tough lately. I feel like I'm fighting a losing battle sometimes.

source_dialogue.speaker: Gail
source_dialogue.text: I know you are, but you're not alone in this fight. We're all here for you and we believe in you. You can beat this, Madalynn. I know you can.

source_dialogue.speaker: Madalynn
source_dialogue.text: I'm trying, Gail. I really am. But it's just so hard sometimes.

source_dialogue.speaker: Gail
source_dialogue.text: I know it is, but you're not alone. We're all here for you and we believe in you. You can do this, Madalynn.

scene.narrative_en: Madalynn discovers new hope when he sees how much his community has rallied behind him. He feels inspired by their support and is determined to fight for his life.

mapped_refs.notes: 시련 is the common Korean term for a challenging struggle.
